linguisticChallenges.msaDialectMixing: MT models frequently produce output that mixes MSA with dialectal Arabic (especially Egyptian or Levantine). In a single paragraph, an MSA verb might appear alongside a colloquial conjunction. This is immediately visible to Arabic readers and reads as incoherent.
linguisticChallenges.rootPatternMorphology: Arabic words derive from three-consonant roots through vowel patterns (e.g., k-t-b → كتب wrote, كتاب book, مكتبة library, كاتب writer). MT must understand that morphologically related forms share semantic connections but have distinct meanings.
linguisticChallenges.bidirectionalText: RTL Arabic with embedded LTR content (variables, URLs, code, numbers) creates complex bidirectional text rendering. Placeholder insertion must respect Unicode bidi algorithm rules. Strings like '{count} ملفات' require careful handling to prevent visual reordering errors.
linguisticChallenges.diacritics: Written Arabic usually omits short vowel diacritics (tashkeel/حركات), creating ambiguity — 'كتب' could be 'wrote' (kataba), 'books' (kutub), or 'was written' (kutiba). MT must disambiguate from context without generating unnecessary diacritics in output (they look pedagogical or Qur'anic in UI text).
linguisticChallenges.genderAgreement: Verbs, adjectives, and demonstratives must agree in gender with their noun. Getting this wrong is one of the most common MT errors in Arabic — e.g., هذه الملف (feminine demonstrative + masculine noun) is immediately wrong.
linguisticChallenges.dualNumber: Arabic distinguishes singular, dual (exactly two), and plural. The dual form affects nouns, adjectives, verbs, and pronouns. This is reflected in the 6 plural categories (zero, one, two, few, many, other). ICU MessageFormat must account for all six.
linguisticChallenges.complexPlurals: Arabic broken plurals (جمع التكسير) change the internal vowel pattern of the word rather than adding a suffix. There is no predictable rule — each noun has its own plural form (كتاب → كتب, قلم → أقلام). MT systems sometimes apply regular plural suffixes instead of the correct broken plural.

dialects.classification: Modern Standard Arabic (MSA) for written/formal use; regional vernaculars are mutually unintelligible between distant groups

encyclopedic.history: Arabic spread with the Islamic conquests of the 7th-8th centuries. MSA is the standardized literary form based on Classical Arabic, used in media, education, and formal contexts across all Arabic-speaking countries. It is one of the six official UN languages.
